turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Today's orders of the Court have been duly entered and certified and filed with the clerk. Justice Thomas has our opinion this morning in case 15-8544, Beckles versus Unites States.

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: This case comes to us on a writ of certiorari to the United States Court of Appeals for the Eleventh Circuit. Petitioner, Travis Beckles was convicted of possession of a firearm by a convicted felon. The district court enhanced his sentence under the sentencing guidelines because his offense qualified as a crime of violence under the residual clause of the career-offender guideline. At the time of petitioner's sentencing, the residual clause defined the crime of violence as any offense that “involves conduct that presents a serious potential risk of physical injury to another.” Petitioner filed a post-conviction motion to vacate his sentence arguing that his offense was not a crime of violence. The district court and the Eleventh Circuit ruled against him and petitioner filed a petition for writ of certiorari to this Court. While his petition was pending, this Court decided Johnson versus United States. In that case, we held that residual clause in one provision of the Armed Career Criminal Act which is worded identically to the residual clause at issue here was unconstitutionally vague. Based on our holding in Johnson, we vacated the judgment of the Court of Appeals and remanded for further consideration. On remand, petitioner argued that the residual clause of the career-offender guideline is unconstitutionally vague under Johnson. The Court of Appeals again affirmed reasoning that Johnson did not say or decide anything about career-offender enhancements under the sentencing guidelines. In an opinion filed with the clerk today, we affirm. We hold that the sentencing guidelines including the residual clause of the career-offender guidelines are not subject to vagueness challenges under the Due Process Clause of the Constitution. Under our vagueness doctrine, laws that fix the sentences for criminal offenses must specify the range of available sentences with sufficient clarity. But the guidelines are advisory and therefore, do not fix the permissible range of sentences. They merely guide the exercise of a court's discretion in choosing a sentence within the statutory range. Congress has long given district courts wide discretion in deciding whether an offender should be incarcerated and for how long. This Court has never suggested that a statute conferring even unfettered discretion on a court to select an appropriate sentence within the statutory range can be vague. If a sentence of unfettered discretion is not unconstitutionally vague then it's difficult to see how a system of guided discretion could be. Moreover, the guidelines do not implicate the twin concerns underlying our vagueness doctrine that is providing sufficient notice and preventing arbitrary enforcement. The statute setting the sentencing range provides all the notice that is required. Similarly, the guidelines do not invite arbitrary enforcement because they do not permit a court to prohibit behavior or to prescribe the available sentencing range. Rather, they merely advise sentencing courts on how to exercise their discretion within the bounds established by Congress. Accordingly, the guidelines cannot be challenged as void for vagueness. For these reasons and other set forth in our opinion, we affirm the judgment of the Eleventh Circuit. Justice Kennedy has filed a concurring opinion. Justice Ginsburg and Justice Sotomayor have filed opinions concurring in the judgment. Justice Kagan took no part in the consideration or decision of this case.
